0: Assessment of grazing management on farm greenhouse gas intensity of beef production systems in the Canadian Prairies using life cycle assessment
Aklilu W. Alemu ² , Henry Janzen , Shannan Little, Xiying Hao, Donald J. Thompson, Vern Baronb Alan Iwaasa , Karen A. Beauchemin, Roland Kröbel

<list>a Lethbridge Research and Development Centre, Lethbridge, AB T1J 4B1, Canada 
b Lacombe Research and Development Centre, Lacombe, AB TL 1W1, Canada 
C Swift Current Research and Development Centre, Swift Current, SK S9H 3X2, Canada</list>

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Beef production Grazing management Holos model Soil carbon Stocking rate|Grazing is a common practice in the beef cattle industry and is an integral component of pasture and rangeland management. The objective of this study was to evaluate impacts of grazing management scenarios on green- house gas (GHG) intensity [kg carbon dioxide equivalents (CO2e) kg 1 beef] at the farm-gate for beef pro- duction systems in western Canada using life cycle assessment. A life cycle assessment over an 8-year period was conducted on a hypothetical but typical beef farm that managed 120 cows, 4 bulls, and their progeny. Calves were backgrounded (raised) on rangeland and market cattle were finished on grain for an average of 134 + 11 d. Four grazing management scenarios were examined: i) light continuous grazing (LC) for all cattle, ii) heavy continuous grazing (HC) for all cattle, iii) light continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle (LCMR), and iv) heavy continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle (HCMR). Greenhouse gas emissions from various sources within the farm were estimated using the whole-farm model, Holos. Soil organic carbon (C) change due to each grazing management scenario was estimated using the Introductory Carbon Balance Model. Primary model inputs came from short- and long-term grazing management studies. Greenhouse gas intensity of beef varied among grazing management scenarios, ranging from 14.5-16.0 kg COe kg- live weight and 24.1-26.6 kg COe kg carcass weight. Greenhouse gas intensity decreased with increasing stocking rate: that of HC grazing management was 9.2% lower than that of LC treatment (14.5 vs 16.0 kg CO2e kg live weight, respectively). Greenhouse gas intensity was similar (< 3%) between LC and LCMR or between HC and HCMR, indicating that the use of moderate rotational grazing for the backgrounding operation in LCMR and HCMR had no effect on overall intensity estimates. However, LCMR management had 7% higher GHG intensity than HCMR (15.6 vs 14.6 kg COe kg live weight, respectively). Average farm production efficiency (kg beef per unit land area) was 17-25% higher for the HC and HCMR grazing management scenarios than the LC and LCMR scenarios. Regardless of grazing management, methane emission from enteric fermentation was the major source of emissions (67-68% of total), followed by nitrous oxide (14-16% of total) from manure management. The rate of soil C sequestration ranged from 0.01 Mg Cha-1yr-1 for rangeland under HC to 0.46 Mg C ha 1 yr for a triticale field used for swath grazing. When soil C sequestration was included in the total emission analysis, GHG intensity estimates decreased by 12-25%, and there was no difference in intensity estimates among the sce- narios. The largest reduction in GHG intensity arising from soil C sequestration was observed for LC (22%) and LCMR (25%) because they sequestered more C than HC and HCMR. Overall, results of our study indicated that grazing management impacted GHG intensity of beef production by influencing diet quality, animal performance and soil C change. It also emphasizes the importance of accounting for all emission sources and sinks within a beef production system when estimating its environmental impacts.
1: Globally, more than 10% of the anthropogenic greenhouse gas

(GHG) emissions (Smith et al., 2007; O'Mara, 2011) come from live- stock farming primarily due to emissions from ruminants (Clark, 2009). The beef cattle industry is under increasing scrutiny by the public

because of its GHG emissions, mainly in the form of enteric methane (CH4, Dyer et al., 2010; Lesschen et al., 2011). Enteric CH4 accounts for more than 53% of total farm emissions for beef production systems in Canada (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Basarab et al., 2012). The Canadian beef cattle industry contributes about 43% of total national agricultural emissions (Environment Canada, 2015).

Management practices such as feed supplements, feeding manage- ment, manure management and improving animal husbandry by en- hancing animal fertility and productivity have been proposed to de- crease GHG emissions from animal agriculture (Hristov et al., 2013). However, manipulation of the soil-plant-animal ecosystem through grazing management and its impact on farm GHG emissions has rarely been studied (Liebig et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2015). Effective grazing management scenarios and innovations in rangeland management allow farmers to better use rangeland and pasture resources for beef production as well as improve rangeland ecosystems, which provide diverse ecological goods and services (Dodds et al., 2008).

Several studies have reported impacts of grazing system and stocking rate on specific farm components such as forage quality and productivity (Pitts and Bryant, 1987; Reeder and Schuman, 2002; Schellenberg et al., 2012), animal productivity (Willms et al., 1986), soil nutrient and water cycling (Baron et al., 2002; Naeth and Chanasyk, 1995; Li et al., 2010; Teague et al., 2011) and soil GHG emissions and soil carbon (C) sequestration (Haferkamp and Macneil, 2004; Soussana et al., 2007, 2010; Liebig et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2014). However, information on the influence of grazing system and stocking rate on total farm GHG emissions is limited. For example, Soussana et al. (2007) measured net ecosystem exchange at nine sites in Europe and reported managed grasslands to be a sink of atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2). However, when C exports (through grazing or harvest) and other emissions including CH4 and nitrous oxide (N20) were included, the same grasslands exhibited net GHG emissions not significantly different from zero. This implies the importance of a whole-farm approach to evaluate impacts of change in farm manage- ment practices aimed at decreasing environmental impacts of beef production systems. The beef cattle industry in North America relies heavily on grazing of rangeland and pasture that sequester soil C through photosynthesis, yet C sequestration is not part of most whole- farm GHG studies (Crosson et al., 2011). In our study, we estimated both GHG emissions and C sequestration for a simulated farm, under various grazing management scenarios.

In Canada, grazing is an important component of feeding practices for the beef cattle industry, especially for cow-calf and backgrounding operations (raising weaned beef cattle in preparation for finishing in a feedlot). More than 80% of Canadian beef cattle farms manage cattle on natural rangeland or seeded pasture during the summer grazing season and 58% practice winter grazing on bales, stockpiled forages or swathed cereal crops (Sheppard et al., 2015). Traditionally, these farms practice either continuous (season-long) grazing or intensive rotational grazing with varying stocking rates (Western Beef Development Centre, 2015). Recommended stocking rates vary by region and type of ran- geland and pasture. For example, in the Canadian Prairies, stocking rates range from 0.22 animal unit months (AUM) for dry mixed- grass prairie regions to 1.6 AUM ha for the foothills fescue prairie regions (Bailey et al., 2010). Animal unit month is the amount of forage required by one animal unit (mature cow weighing 453.6 kg) for one month based on a forage allowance of 11.8 kg per day. Several studies (e.g., Willms et al., 1986; Naeth et al., 1991; Manley et al., 1997; Li et al., 2012) have evaluated impacts of grazing strategy (continuous, rotational) and stocking rate for beef production systems, but their results were inconclusive. For example, Willms et al. (1986) reported that increasing stocking rate reduced forage production and weight gain per animal but increased total weight gain per unit area. However, Pitts and Bryant (1987) reported no difference in steer performance as well as forage quality and availability between continuous and rota- tional grazing with different stocking rates.

The objective of our study was to evaluate impacts of grazing management strategy and stocking rate on farm GHG intensity [kg CO2 equivalents (CO2e) kg beef of Canadian beef production using life cycle assessment (LCA). Primary data from short-and long-term grazing studies were used to evaluate four grazing management scenarios: i) light continuous grazing (LC) for all cattle (low stocking rate), ii) heavy continuous grazing (HC) for all cattle (heavy stocking rate, the most commonly-used grazing management strategy; Smith and Hoppe, 2000), iii) light continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle (LCMR), and iv) heavy continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle (HCMR). A deferred-rotational system that involves dividing the rangeland into at least two paddocks, each of which receives deferment, was used as the rotational grazing strategy. In all scenarios, cow-calf pairs grazed natural rangeland (Rough Fescue Prairie), while backgrounded cattle grazed re-established rangeland (established in 2001 by seeding a mixture of native cool- and warm- season grasses, native legume and native shrub species).

6: The study used International Organization for Standardization (ISO)-compliant LCA to compare cradle-to-farm gate cumulative GHG emissions associated with beef production systems practicing different grazing management scenarios. Thus, all emissions related to produc- tion of feed and beef until the time that animals left the farm (farm- gate) were included, but emissions related to transport of animals to slaughter or subsequent processing and transport to the consumer were excluded. Furthermore, energy use emissions associated with acquisi- tion of raw materials and manufacturing of machinery and emissions related to buildings were not included. All emissions were expressed as COe, where: CO2 = 1, N2O = 265 and CH4 = 28 on a mass basis (Myhre et al., 2013). Results were expressed as GHG intensity calcu- lated as total farm GHG emissions per unit beef (live weight or carcass weight). Because soil C change varies among sites, farm GHG intensity was estimated with and without soil C change estimates to show the range of possible responses.
7: <header>2.2. Description of beef production system and simulated farm </header>

Beef production in Canada is a three-phase system with cow-calf, backgrounding and finishing operations (Sheppard et al., 2015; Western Beef Development Centre, 2015). The cow-calf operations maintain breeding stock that includes mature cows, breeding bulls, replacement heifers and newborn calves (Table 1). After weaning, some of the progeny are maintained on the farm to replace culled cows, whereas others are moved to backgrounding and/or finishing opera- tions. Backgrounding is a period of growth between weaning and fin- ishing, when weaned calves are further grown on high-forage diets and/ or rangeland before entering the finishing phase. For commercial beef operations, the length of backgrounding varies with animal weight gain, market prices of feed and cattle and feed availability and quality (Sheppard et al., 2015). In our study, calves weaned in the fall entered the finishing phase after they were backgrounded on forage-based diets (mixed hay) over winter followed by grazing on re-established range- land the following summer (Fig. 1). In the finishing phase, animals received a high-grain diet until slaughter. Although grazing manage- ment scenarios studied differed only in the cow-calf and backgrounding operations, we included the finishing operation as well to capture im- pacts of rangeland management throughout the entire production cycle.

management on farm GHG intensity, similar to the approach of Beauchemin et al. (2010). The simulated farm was located in Vulcan County, southern Alberta, Canada (Ecodistrict 799; Marshall et al., 1999). The farm consisted of a beef operation with 120 cows, four bulls and their progeny, a cropping operation, a hay production operation and rangeland area for grazing. The LCA for beef cattle covered an 8- year period to represent the life span of the breeding herd within the beef production cycle, thereby accounting for the lifetime GHG emis- sions of cows and bulls (Table 1). Since details of this approach are provided in Beauchemin et al. (2010), only a brief description is pro- vided here. The beef LCA started with the birth of breeding stock (120 heifers and four bulls) and ended 8 years later with the birth of

replacement animals and slaughter of the initial breeding stock (Bailey, 1991; Table 1). With an 85% annual calving rate (the percentage of cows producing calves that survived to weaning), calves were born in April with mean weight of 39 kg (Sheppard et al., 2015) and weaned at the age of 7 months (end of October) with mean weight of 214 + 14 kg (Table 2). The sex ratio of calves was assumed to be 1:1 (Bailey, 1991) and 102 calves were weaned each year. Cows were managed with good quality hay [meadow bromegrass (Bromus riparius Rehm.) alfalfa (Medicago sativa L.) mix] and bedding during the cold/ pre-calving season (February-April), on natural rangeland (Rough Fescue Prairie) during the warm season (May-October) and on swathed triticale (Triticosecale Wittmack) for the remaining three months

(November-January, Fig. 1). Triticale was seeded in May or June, swathed at the soft dough stage at the end of September and left in the field for the late-fall and early-winter grazing (McKenzie et al., 2011; Baron et al., 2014). New born calves suckled milk from their mothers and had access to hay in April and cow milk and rangeland forage until weaning. Heifers required to replace culled cows in the breeding stock were fed good quality mixed hay for 6 months post-weaning; they were bred at 15 months and gave birth at 24 months.

Weaned steers and heifers (n = 99) were backgrounded in an out- door pen for 166 days on good quality mixed hay with average daily gain (ADG) of 0.65 kg d 1 followed by an additional 120 days on re- established rangeland with ADG of 0.78 + 0.1 kg d 1 before they moved to the finishing operation (Fig. 1; Table 2.). After entering the finishing stage at a mean body weight of 413 + 15 kg, steers and heifers (n = 98) were fed a finishing diet containing 90% barley (Hordeum vulgare L.) grain, 8% barley silage and 2% mineral and vi- tamin supplements (dry matter (DM) basis; NRC, 2000) to attain an ADG of 1.6 kg Steers and heifers were slaughtered at mean market weight of 630 kg, at the age of 20.6 + 0.05 months for LC and LCMR and 21.1 + 0.01 months for HC and HCMR (Table 1).

a LC = light continuous grazing, HC = heavy continuous grazing, LCMR = light continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle, HCMR = heavy continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle. A deferred-rotational system was used as the rotational grazing strategy. b Pasture was established in 2001 by seeding a mixture of native cool-season grass, native legume species, native warm-season grass and native shrub species (Schellenberg et al., 2012).

(November-January, Fig. 1). Triticale was seeded in May or June, swathed at the soft dough stage at the end of September and left in the field for the late-fall and early-winter grazing (McKenzie et al., 2011; Baron et al., 2014). New born calves suckled milk from their mothers and had access to hay in April and cow milk and rangeland forage until weaning. Heifers required to replace culled cows in the breeding stock were fed good quality mixed hay for 6 months post-weaning; they were bred at 15 months and gave birth at 24 months.

Weaned steers and heifers (n = 99) were backgrounded in an out- door pen for 166 days on good quality mixed hay with average daily gain (ADG) of 0.65 kg d 1 followed by an additional 120 days on re- established rangeland with ADG of 0.78 + 0.1 kg d 1 before they moved to the finishing operation (Fig. 1; Table 2.). After entering the finishing stage at a mean body weight of 413 + 15 kg, steers and heifers (n = 98) were fed a finishing diet containing 90% barley (Hordeum vulgare L.) grain, 8% barley silage and 2% mineral and vi- tamin supplements (dry matter (DM) basis; NRC, 2000) to attain an ADG of 1.6 kg Steers and heifers were slaughtered at mean market weight of 630 kg, at the age of 20.6 + 0.05 months for LC and LCMR and 21.1 + 0.01 months for HC and HCMR (Table 1).

a Mixed hay consisted of meadow bromegrass, Kentucky bluegrass and < 30% alfalfa (Basarab et al., 2012). Re-established rangeland used for backgrounded cattle was established in 2001 by seeding a mixture of native cool-season grass, native legume species, native warm-season grass and native shrub species (Schellenberg et al., 2012). b Number of days required for finishing (134 days) to reach the target market weight of 630 kg was an average value and varied among grazing management scenarios: 121, 144, 129 and 144 days for LC, HC, LCMR and HCMR scenarios, respectively.

management on farm GHG intensity, similar to the approach of Beauchemin et al. (2010). The simulated farm was located in Vulcan County, southern Alberta, Canada (Ecodistrict 799; Marshall et al., 1999). The farm consisted of a beef operation with 120 cows, four bulls and their progeny, a cropping operation, a hay production operation and rangeland area for grazing. The LCA for beef cattle covered an 8- year period to represent the life span of the breeding herd within the beef production cycle, thereby accounting for the lifetime GHG emis- sions of cows and bulls (Table 1). Since details of this approach are provided in Beauchemin et al. (2010), only a brief description is pro- vided here. The beef LCA started with the birth of breeding stock (120 heifers and four bulls) and ended 8 years later with the birth of

replacement animals and slaughter of the initial breeding stock (Bailey, 1991; Table 1). With an 85% annual calving rate (the percentage of cows producing calves that survived to weaning), calves were born in April with mean weight of 39 kg (Sheppard et al., 2015) and weaned at the age of 7 months (end of October) with mean weight of 214 + 14 kg (Table 2). The sex ratio of calves was assumed to be 1:1 (Bailey, 1991) and 102 calves were weaned each year. Cows were managed with good quality hay [meadow bromegrass (Bromus riparius Rehm.) alfalfa (Medicago sativa L.) mix] and bedding during the cold/ pre-calving season (February-April), on natural rangeland (Rough Fescue Prairie) during the warm season (May-October) and on swathed triticale (Triticosecale Wittmack) for the remaining three months

a Mixed hay consisted of meadow bromegrass, Kentucky bluegrass and < 30% alfalfa (Basarab et al., 2012). Re-established rangeland used for backgrounded cattle was established in 2001 by seeding a mixture of native cool-season grass, native legume species, native warm-season grass and native shrub species (Schellenberg et al., 2012). b Number of days required for finishing (134 days) to reach the target market weight of 630 kg was an average value and varied among grazing management scenarios: 121, 144, 129 and 144 days for LC, HC, LCMR and HCMR scenarios, respectively.

management on farm GHG intensity, similar to the approach of Beauchemin et al. (2010). The simulated farm was located in Vulcan County, southern Alberta, Canada (Ecodistrict 799; Marshall et al., 1999). The farm consisted of a beef operation with 120 cows, four bulls and their progeny, a cropping operation, a hay production operation and rangeland area for grazing. The LCA for beef cattle covered an 8- year period to represent the life span of the breeding herd within the beef production cycle, thereby accounting for the lifetime GHG emis- sions of cows and bulls (Table 1). Since details of this approach are provided in Beauchemin et al. (2010), only a brief description is pro- vided here. The beef LCA started with the birth of breeding stock (120 heifers and four bulls) and ended 8 years later with the birth of

replacement animals and slaughter of the initial breeding stock (Bailey, 1991; Table 1). With an 85% annual calving rate (the percentage of cows producing calves that survived to weaning), calves were born in April with mean weight of 39 kg (Sheppard et al., 2015) and weaned at the age of 7 months (end of October) with mean weight of 214 + 14 kg (Table 2). The sex ratio of calves was assumed to be 1:1 (Bailey, 1991) and 102 calves were weaned each year. Cows were managed with good quality hay [meadow bromegrass (Bromus riparius Rehm.) alfalfa (Medicago sativa L.) mix] and bedding during the cold/ pre-calving season (February-April), on natural rangeland (Rough Fescue Prairie) during the warm season (May-October) and on swathed triticale (Triticosecale Wittmack) for the remaining three months

a LC = light continuous grazing, HC = heavy continuous grazing, LCMR = light continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle, HCMR = heavy continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle. A deferred-rotational system was used as the rotational grazing strategy. b Pasture was established in 2001 by seeding a mixture of native cool-season grass, native legume species, native warm-season grass and native shrub species (Schellenberg et al., 2012).

(November-January, Fig. 1). Triticale was seeded in May or June, swathed at the soft dough stage at the end of September and left in the field for the late-fall and early-winter grazing (McKenzie et al., 2011; Baron et al., 2014). New born calves suckled milk from their mothers and had access to hay in April and cow milk and rangeland forage until weaning. Heifers required to replace culled cows in the breeding stock were fed good quality mixed hay for 6 months post-weaning; they were bred at 15 months and gave birth at 24 months.

Weaned steers and heifers (n = 99) were backgrounded in an out- door pen for 166 days on good quality mixed hay with average daily gain (ADG) of 0.65 kg d 1 followed by an additional 120 days on re- established rangeland with ADG of 0.78 + 0.1 kg d 1 before they moved to the finishing operation (Fig. 1; Table 2.). After entering the finishing stage at a mean body weight of 413 + 15 kg, steers and heifers (n = 98) were fed a finishing diet containing 90% barley (Hordeum vulgare L.) grain, 8% barley silage and 2% mineral and vi- tamin supplements (dry matter (DM) basis; NRC, 2000) to attain an ADG of 1.6 kg Steers and heifers were slaughtered at mean market weight of 630 kg, at the age of 20.6 + 0.05 months for LC and LCMR and 21.1 + 0.01 months for HC and HCMR (Table 1).

a LC = light continuous grazing, HC = heavy continuous grazing, LCMR = light continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle, HCMR = heavy continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle. A deferred-rotational system was used as the rotational grazing strategy. b Pasture was established in 2001 by seeding a mixture of native cool-season grass, native legume species, native warm-season grass and native shrub species (Schellenberg et al., 2012).

(November-January, Fig. 1). Triticale was seeded in May or June, swathed at the soft dough stage at the end of September and left in the field for the late-fall and early-winter grazing (McKenzie et al., 2011; Baron et al., 2014). New born calves suckled milk from their mothers and had access to hay in April and cow milk and rangeland forage until weaning. Heifers required to replace culled cows in the breeding stock were fed good quality mixed hay for 6 months post-weaning; they were bred at 15 months and gave birth at 24 months.

Weaned steers and heifers (n = 99) were backgrounded in an out- door pen for 166 days on good quality mixed hay with average daily gain (ADG) of 0.65 kg d 1 followed by an additional 120 days on re- established rangeland with ADG of 0.78 + 0.1 kg d 1 before they moved to the finishing operation (Fig. 1; Table 2.). After entering the finishing stage at a mean body weight of 413 + 15 kg, steers and heifers (n = 98) were fed a finishing diet containing 90% barley (Hordeum vulgare L.) grain, 8% barley silage and 2% mineral and vi- tamin supplements (dry matter (DM) basis; NRC, 2000) to attain an ADG of 1.6 kg Steers and heifers were slaughtered at mean market weight of 630 kg, at the age of 20.6 + 0.05 months for LC and LCMR and 21.1 + 0.01 months for HC and HCMR (Table 1).
19: The four grazing management scenarios (LC, HC, LCMR, HCMR, Table 3) evaluated in the current LCA were based on data from long- and short-term grazing studies (Tables 4 and 5). Data on the cow-calf pairs grazing on natural rangeland were obtained from a long-term grazing study 60 years) established in 1949 at Agriculture and Agri- Food Canada's grassland research substation, Stavely, Alberta, Canada (Willms et al., 1986, 2012). The study investigated the carrying capa- city of Rough Fescue Prairie and the response of livestock and vegeta- tion to continuous grazing at two stocking rates: light (1.2 AUM ha- or 5 ha per cow-calf pair) and heavy (2.4 AUM ha 1 or 2.5 ha per cow- calf pair, Table 3). The recommended stocking rate for the area (Foothills Fescue Prairie ecoregions) ranged from 1.0-1.6 AUM ha-1 (Bailey et al., 2010). Cow-calf pairs were continuously grazed on nat- ural rangeland from May-October (Fig. 1). The experimental site is

located in foothills of the Rocky Mountains with a dry sub-humid cli- mate and annual precipitation of 502 mm (10-year average, 1997-2006), with 303 mm from April-July (Li et al., 2012). Mean an- nual and grazing season (May to October) temperature is 5.5 and 11.6 respectively. The natural rangeland contained predominantly foothills rough fescue grass (Festuca carnpestris Rydb.) in lightly grazed areas and parry oat grass (Danthonia parryi Scribn.) and bluebunch fescue (Festuca idahoensis Elmer) in heavily grazed areas (Dormaar and Willms, 1990). Data (Tables 4, 5) on soil characteristics (C, N, bulk density, temperature, and moisture), forage production, utilization rate and quality [neutral detergent fiber (NDF), acid detergent fiber (ADF), crude protein (CP)] and animal performance obtained from Johnston (1961, 1962), Johnston et al. (1971), Dormaar et al. (1977), Willms et al. (1986), Dormaar and Willms (1992), Li et al. (2009, 2010, 2012). The mean forage utilization rates (the percentage of annual forage production that is removed by animals throughout a grazing season) were 27% and 50% for the light and heavy stocking rates, respectively. Forage utilization rate for the heavy stocking rate on natural rangeland (50%) was maintained within the range of ecologically sustainable rate of utilization (25-50%) that allows carryover of biomass to maintain other ecological functions (Adams et al., 2013). The total area of nat- ural rangeland required to support breeding stock (cow-calf pairs, bulls, replacement stock) during the 8-year life cycle was estimated based on DM intake and available forage yield and utilization rate (Table 4). Further details on the long-term grazing study at Stavely have been reported by Willms et al. (1986, 2012), Willms (1988), Dormaar and Willms (1990, 2000), Douwes and Willms (2012) and Li et al. (2009, 2012).

Following summer grazing on natural rangeland, beef cows had access to swathed triticale for 92 days, until they moved to a drylot for calving (Fig. 1). Data on swath grazing of beef cows on triticale were obtained from McKenzie et al. (2011) and Baron et al. (2014). Triticale was selected for swath grazing due to its higher forage production and utilization, ability to sustain a high level of animal performance and low total feeding cost (Baron et al., 2014). Data (Tables 4 and 5) on soil characteristics, crop establishment, yield, nutritive value, carrying ca- pacity and animal performance obtained from McKenzie et al. (2011)

Grazing management scenarios|Stocking rate (AUM ha|Stocking rate (AUM ha Grazing management scenarios|Cow-calf pairs (grazed natural rangeland)|Backgrounded cattle (grazed re-established rangeland) Light continuous (LC), low stocking rate|1.2|1.4 Heavy continuous (HC), high stocking rate|2.4|2.8 Light continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle (LCMR)|1.2|1.8 Heavy continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle (HCMR)|2.4|1.8

a AUM = animal unit month, which is the amount of forage required by one animal unit for one month. One animal unit is defined as one mature cow of approximately 453.6 kg, either dry or with her suckling calf, with an average daily forage consumption of 11.8 per day (California Rangelands Research and Information Center, 1995). b A deferred-rotational system was used as the rotational grazing strategy. c Data on natural rangeland used for the cow-calf operation were obtained from a long-term study (> 60 years) conducted at Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada's rangeland research substation near Stavely, AB, Canada. The study began in 1949 to determine the carrying capacity of Rough Fescue Prairie (Douwes and Willms, 2012). d Established in 2001 by seeding a mixture of native cool-season grass, native legume species, native warm-season grass and native shrub species. Data on re-established rangeland used for the backgrounded cattle were obtained from a 9-year (2002-2011) study at Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada's Semiarid Prairie Agricultural Research Center near Swift Current, SK, Canada (Schellenberg et al., 2012).

and Baron et al. (2014). Briefly, triticale was seeded from May 25 to June 12 with N fertilizer (urea, 72 kg ha and P2O5 (30 and swathed at the soft dough stage at the end of September (McKenzie et al., 2011). Average yield and utilization rate were 8.9 Mg DM ha and 84%, respectively (Table 4).

The simulated farm backgrounded steers and heifers by feeding mixed hay during winter followed by access to re-established rangeland in the following year before moving them to the finishing operation (Fig. 1). Data on backgrounded cattle grazed on re-established range- land obtained from a 10-year (2002-2011) grazing study (Iwaasa and Schellenberg, 2005; Iwaasa et al., 2012, 2016; Schellenberg et al., 2012). Briefly, in 2000 mixed native grass was seeded on land cropped since the 1920's, and the grazing study was conducted from 2002 to 2011 (Iwaasa et al., 2012; Schellenberg et al., 2012). Three grazing management scenarios were evaluated: continuous grazing with light (1.4 and heavy (2.8 AUM ha-1 stocking rates as well as rotational grazing (deferred-rotation) with moderate stocking rate (1.8 AUM ha-1, Table 3). Grazing started at different seasons (spring, summer and fall) in the rotational grazing system (deferred-rotation), but over the course of three years it would have occurred during each season (e.g., year1 = spring, year2 = summer, year3 = fall). The study collected detailed data on forage production, utilization and quality; animal performance and soil characteristics (Iwaasa and Schellenberg, 2005; Iwaasa et al., 2012). Forage utilization rate was 49% for light stocking, 67% for heavy stocking and 53% for deferred-rotational grazing (Table 4).

Data on mixed hay [meadow bromegrass, Kentucky bluegrass (Poa pratensis) and alfalfa 30%)] production, used for drylot feeding (November-April), were obtained from Basarab et al. (2012). Average fertilizer application rates per ha were 100 kg N as urea, 30 kg P2O5 and 30 kg K2O. The amount of land required for hay production was esti- mated based on DM intake, land productivity (6.7 Mg DM and losses related to harvest (12%) and feeding (20%) (Rotz and Muck, 1994). Data on production of barley silage and grain used in the fin- ishing operation were derived from McCartney and Vaage (1994), McKenzie et al. (2004) and Basarab et al. (2012).

Grazing management scenarios|Stocking rate (AUM ha|Stocking rate (AUM ha Grazing management scenarios|Cow-calf pairs (grazed natural rangeland)|Backgrounded cattle (grazed re-established rangeland) Light continuous (LC), low stocking rate|1.2|1.4 Heavy continuous (HC), high stocking rate|2.4|2.8 Light continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle (LCMR)|1.2|1.8 Heavy continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle (HCMR)|2.4|1.8

a AUM = animal unit month, which is the amount of forage required by one animal unit for one month. One animal unit is defined as one mature cow of approximately 453.6 kg, either dry or with her suckling calf, with an average daily forage consumption of 11.8 per day (California Rangelands Research and Information Center, 1995). b A deferred-rotational system was used as the rotational grazing strategy. c Data on natural rangeland used for the cow-calf operation were obtained from a long-term study (> 60 years) conducted at Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada's rangeland research substation near Stavely, AB, Canada. The study began in 1949 to determine the carrying capacity of Rough Fescue Prairie (Douwes and Willms, 2012). d Established in 2001 by seeding a mixture of native cool-season grass, native legume species, native warm-season grass and native shrub species. Data on re-established rangeland used for the backgrounded cattle were obtained from a 9-year (2002-2011) study at Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada's Semiarid Prairie Agricultural Research Center near Swift Current, SK, Canada (Schellenberg et al., 2012).

and Baron et al. (2014). Briefly, triticale was seeded from May 25 to June 12 with N fertilizer (urea, 72 kg ha and P2O5 (30 and swathed at the soft dough stage at the end of September (McKenzie et al., 2011). Average yield and utilization rate were 8.9 Mg DM ha and 84%, respectively (Table 4).

The simulated farm backgrounded steers and heifers by feeding mixed hay during winter followed by access to re-established rangeland in the following year before moving them to the finishing operation (Fig. 1). Data on backgrounded cattle grazed on re-established range- land obtained from a 10-year (2002-2011) grazing study (Iwaasa and Schellenberg, 2005; Iwaasa et al., 2012, 2016; Schellenberg et al., 2012). Briefly, in 2000 mixed native grass was seeded on land cropped since the 1920's, and the grazing study was conducted from 2002 to 2011 (Iwaasa et al., 2012; Schellenberg et al., 2012). Three grazing management scenarios were evaluated: continuous grazing with light (1.4 and heavy (2.8 AUM ha-1 stocking rates as well as rotational grazing (deferred-rotation) with moderate stocking rate (1.8 AUM ha-1, Table 3). Grazing started at different seasons (spring, summer and fall) in the rotational grazing system (deferred-rotation), but over the course of three years it would have occurred during each season (e.g., year1 = spring, year2 = summer, year3 = fall). The study collected detailed data on forage production, utilization and quality; animal performance and soil characteristics (Iwaasa and Schellenberg, 2005; Iwaasa et al., 2012). Forage utilization rate was 49% for light stocking, 67% for heavy stocking and 53% for deferred-rotational grazing (Table 4).

Data on mixed hay [meadow bromegrass, Kentucky bluegrass (Poa pratensis) and alfalfa 30%)] production, used for drylot feeding (November-April), were obtained from Basarab et al. (2012). Average fertilizer application rates per ha were 100 kg N as urea, 30 kg P2O5 and 30 kg K2O. The amount of land required for hay production was esti- mated based on DM intake, land productivity (6.7 Mg DM and losses related to harvest (12%) and feeding (20%) (Rotz and Muck, 1994). Data on production of barley silage and grain used in the fin- ishing operation were derived from McCartney and Vaage (1994), McKenzie et al. (2004) and Basarab et al. (2012).
23: The four grazing management scenarios (LC, HC, LCMR, HCMR, Table 3) evaluated in the current LCA were based on data from long- and short-term grazing studies (Tables 4 and 5). Data on the cow-calf pairs grazing on natural rangeland were obtained from a long-term grazing study 60 years) established in 1949 at Agriculture and Agri- Food Canada's grassland research substation, Stavely, Alberta, Canada (Willms et al., 1986, 2012). The study investigated the carrying capa- city of Rough Fescue Prairie and the response of livestock and vegeta- tion to continuous grazing at two stocking rates: light (1.2 AUM ha- or 5 ha per cow-calf pair) and heavy (2.4 AUM ha 1 or 2.5 ha per cow- calf pair, Table 3). The recommended stocking rate for the area (Foothills Fescue Prairie ecoregions) ranged from 1.0-1.6 AUM ha-1 (Bailey et al., 2010). Cow-calf pairs were continuously grazed on nat- ural rangeland from May-October (Fig. 1). The experimental site is

located in foothills of the Rocky Mountains with a dry sub-humid cli- mate and annual precipitation of 502 mm (10-year average, 1997-2006), with 303 mm from April-July (Li et al., 2012). Mean an- nual and grazing season (May to October) temperature is 5.5 and 11.6 respectively. The natural rangeland contained predominantly foothills rough fescue grass (Festuca carnpestris Rydb.) in lightly grazed areas and parry oat grass (Danthonia parryi Scribn.) and bluebunch fescue (Festuca idahoensis Elmer) in heavily grazed areas (Dormaar and Willms, 1990). Data (Tables 4, 5) on soil characteristics (C, N, bulk density, temperature, and moisture), forage production, utilization rate and quality [neutral detergent fiber (NDF), acid detergent fiber (ADF), crude protein (CP)] and animal performance obtained from Johnston (1961, 1962), Johnston et al. (1971), Dormaar et al. (1977), Willms et al. (1986), Dormaar and Willms (1992), Li et al. (2009, 2010, 2012). The mean forage utilization rates (the percentage of annual forage production that is removed by animals throughout a grazing season) were 27% and 50% for the light and heavy stocking rates, respectively. Forage utilization rate for the heavy stocking rate on natural rangeland (50%) was maintained within the range of ecologically sustainable rate of utilization (25-50%) that allows carryover of biomass to maintain other ecological functions (Adams et al., 2013). The total area of nat- ural rangeland required to support breeding stock (cow-calf pairs, bulls, replacement stock) during the 8-year life cycle was estimated based on DM intake and available forage yield and utilization rate (Table 4). Further details on the long-term grazing study at Stavely have been reported by Willms et al. (1986, 2012), Willms (1988), Dormaar and Willms (1990, 2000), Douwes and Willms (2012) and Li et al. (2009, 2012).

Following summer grazing on natural rangeland, beef cows had access to swathed triticale for 92 days, until they moved to a drylot for calving (Fig. 1). Data on swath grazing of beef cows on triticale were obtained from McKenzie et al. (2011) and Baron et al. (2014). Triticale was selected for swath grazing due to its higher forage production and utilization, ability to sustain a high level of animal performance and low total feeding cost (Baron et al., 2014). Data (Tables 4 and 5) on soil characteristics, crop establishment, yield, nutritive value, carrying ca- pacity and animal performance obtained from McKenzie et al. (2011)

One animal unit is defined as one mature cow of approximately 453.6 kg, either dry or with her suckling calf, with an average daily forage consumption of 11.8 per day (California Rangelands Research and Information Center, 1995). b A deferred-rotational system was used as the rotational grazing strategy. c Data on natural rangeland used for the cow-calf operation were obtained from a long-term study (> 60 years) conducted at Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada's rangeland research substation near Stavely, AB, Canada. The study began in 1949 to determine the carrying capacity of Rough Fescue Prairie (Douwes and Willms, 2012). d Established in 2001 by seeding a mixture of native cool-season grass, native legume species, native warm-season grass and native shrub species. Data on re-established rangeland used for the backgrounded cattle were obtained from a 9-year (2002-2011) study at Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada's Semiarid Prairie Agricultural Research Center near Swift Current, SK, Canada (Schellenberg et al., 2012).

and Baron et al. (2014). Briefly, triticale was seeded from May 25 to June 12 with N fertilizer (urea, 72 kg ha and P2O5 (30 and swathed at the soft dough stage at the end of September (McKenzie et al., 2011). Average yield and utilization rate were 8.9 Mg DM ha and 84%, respectively (Table 4).

The simulated farm backgrounded steers and heifers by feeding mixed hay during winter followed by access to re-established rangeland in the following year before moving them to the finishing operation (Fig. 1). Data on backgrounded cattle grazed on re-established range- land obtained from a 10-year (2002-2011) grazing study (Iwaasa and Schellenberg, 2005; Iwaasa et al., 2012, 2016; Schellenberg et al., 2012). Briefly, in 2000 mixed native grass was seeded on land cropped since the 1920's, and the grazing study was conducted from 2002 to 2011 (Iwaasa et al., 2012; Schellenberg et al., 2012). Three grazing management scenarios were evaluated: continuous grazing with light (1.4 and heavy (2.8 AUM ha-1 stocking rates as well as rotational grazing (deferred-rotation) with moderate stocking rate (1.8 AUM ha-1, Table 3). Grazing started at different seasons (spring, summer and fall) in the rotational grazing system (deferred-rotation), but over the course of three years it would have occurred during each season (e.g., year1 = spring, year2 = summer, year3 = fall). The study collected detailed data on forage production, utilization and quality; animal performance and soil characteristics (Iwaasa and Schellenberg, 2005; Iwaasa et al., 2012). Forage utilization rate was 49% for light stocking, 67% for heavy stocking and 53% for deferred-rotational grazing (Table 4).

Data on mixed hay [meadow bromegrass, Kentucky bluegrass (Poa pratensis) and alfalfa 30%)] production, used for drylot feeding (November-April), were obtained from Basarab et al. (2012). Average fertilizer application rates per ha were 100 kg N as urea, 30 kg P2O5 and 30 kg K2O. The amount of land required for hay production was esti- mated based on DM intake, land productivity (6.7 Mg DM and losses related to harvest (12%) and feeding (20%) (Rotz and Muck, 1994). Data on production of barley silage and grain used in the fin- ishing operation were derived from McCartney and Vaage (1994), McKenzie et al. (2004) and Basarab et al. (2012).
27: <header>2.4. Prediction of farm greenhouse gas emissions </header>

Greenhouse gas emissions from the farm were estimated using Holos Research (V2.2, www.agr.gc.ca/holos-ghg), a farm-scale model devel- oped by Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada (Little et al., 2008). Holos is an empirical model based on the Intergovernmental Pannel on Climate Change (IPCC) Tier 2 methodology (IPCC, 2006) modified for Canadian

production systems. It predicts GHG emissions at a monthly time step for livestock operations and at an annual time step for cropping systems as well as for land use and management changes. Ecodistrict-level emission factors adjusted for variations in climatic and edaphic condi- tions across Canada are incorporated into the model (Table 6). The model is well-documented (Little et al., 2008) and has been applied to estimate farm GHG intensity from beef (Beauchemin et al., 2010, 2011; Bonesmo et al., 2013; Hünerberg et al., 2014) and dairy production systems (McGeough et al., 2012; Bonesmo et al., 2013) as well as to evaluate impacts of change in management practices on farm GHG emissions (Beauchemin et al., 2011).

Predicted total farm GHG emissions included: enteric CH4 emissions from rumen fermentation; CH4 and N2O emissions from manure; N2O emissions from on-farm soils and cropping; N20 emissions from N leaching, runoff and volatilization (indirect N20) off-farm; and CO2 emissions from on-farm energy use and off-farm production of farm inputs. On-farm CO2 emissions or C sequestration due to soil C change were predicted using the Introductory Carbon Balance Model (ICBM, Andrén and Kätterer, 1997) using the simulation approach described by Kröbel et al., 2016 (Table 6).

29: Enteric CH4 was estimated for individual cattle categories according to the IPCC Tier 2 method (IPCC, 2006). Estimation of gross energy intake of the animals at different stages of production, considering the energy density of diets, was based on net energy requirements, a pro- duct of energy expenditures for maintenance, activity, growth, preg- nancy and lactation. Measured feed quality (Table 5) values were used as inputs for the model. Holos predicts enteric CH4 emissions from gross energy intake using CH4 conversion factors (Ym, Table 5) divided by the energy content of CH4 (55.65 MJ kg - 1, Table 6). Values of Ym for the forage-based diet obtained from IPCC (2006) Tier 2 method (Little et al., 2008) and those for the high grain barley-based diet for finishing cattle obtained from Beauchemin and McGinn (2005). The model pre- dicted enteric CH4 emissions from calves on rangeland assuming that their DM consumption equaled 1% of their body weight (Little et al., 2008). Methane emissions from manure management were estimated based on the concentration of volatile solids (VS) produced, which is based on the gross energy intake of the animal and diet digestibility (IPCC, 2006). Finally CH4 was calculated as: VS excreted (kg VS head day X B (CH4 producing capacity, 0.19 m³ CH4 kg - VS) X CH4 conversion factor (0.01 for rangeland grazing and 0.17 for deep bedding) X conversion factor from volume to mass (0.67 Table 6).

c Re-established rangeland was established in 2001 by seeding a mixture of native cool- season grass, native legume species, native warm-season grass and native shrub species. Data on re-established rangeland used for the backgrounded cattle were obtained from a 9-year (2002-2011) study at Agriculture and Agri-Food Canada's Semiarid Prairie Agricultural Research Center near Swift Current, SK, Canada (Iwaasa and Schellenberg, 2005; Schellenberg et al., 2012; Iwaasa et al., 2012, 2016). 
d Application rate of nitrogen fertilizer on triticale, barley silage and grain and mixed hay were obtained from Basarab et al. (2012) and Baron et al. (2014). Mixed hay con- sisted of meadow bromegrass, Kentucky bluegrass and < 30% alfalfa (Basarab et al., 2012). 
e Estimate the amount of land required given dry matter intake of beef cattle, land productivity, forage utilization rate and loss due to harvest and feeding for hay and barley silage and grain.</list>
34: Holos calculated soil-generated direct N2O from total N inputs using the Canada specific algorithm used in the national inventory (Rochette et al., 2008; Table 6). Total N input from synthetic fertilizer, land-ap- plied manure, and above- and below-ground residue decomposition was multiplied by an N2O emission factor adjusted for growing season precipitation and potential evapotranspiration, soil texture, tillage and topography of the ecodistrict (Rochette et al., 2008). Fertilizer N input was estimated based on crop N requirement (Table 6), but took manure N into account by assuming that all manure produced on-farm was applied to land used to produce cereal crops (barley, triticale). Above- and below-ground residue yields were estimated using crop yield, crop moisture content and the yield-to-residue ratio (Janzen et al., 2003). The amount of plant-available N generated by soil mineralization was estimated from the change in soil C, assuming a C:N ratio in organic matter of 10:1 (Little et al., 2008).
Direct N2O emissions from manure storage were calculated from manure N content and emission factors for the farm's manure handling

systems, deep bedding for drylot feeding and feces and urine deposited on rangeland during grazing (Table 6). Manure N content was calcu- lated from animal N retention (NRC, 2000), diet CP and DM intake. DM intake for each animal category was estimated using NRC (2000) as intake required to sustain maintenance and the target level of energy retention (pregnancy, growth or lactation) based on animal require- ments and feed characteristics.

Indirect N2O emissions due to N leaching, runoff, and volatilization were estimated using emission factors from IPCC (2006) and the as- sumed fraction of N lost from fertilizer, manure and residues (Table 6). Fraction of leaching was estimated using a Canada specific algorithm that accounts for precipitation and evapotranspiration (Rochette et al., 2008).

36: Soil C change was predicted using ICBM, a dynamic two-component first-order kinetic model (Andrén and Kätterer, 1997). The model has been applied to assess the dynamics of agricultural soil C pools in Ca- nada (Bolinder et al., 2008; Lemke et al., 2010) and was recently configured for integration into Holos (Kröbel et al., 2016). Parameters of the model include young (Y) and old (O) soil C pools, input of total C from crop residues [above-ground (iag) and below-ground (ibg)], C input from applied manure (im), decomposition constants for the young (ky) and old (ko) C pools, a humification coefficient (h) that determines the fraction that enters the old pool and an external farm specific factor (re Table 6). Uncertainty analysis of ICBM`s input data was not performed. The re factor (0.82) accounts for relative effects of soil moisture, tem- perature and cultivation (Bolinder et al., 2007). Total soil organic C is the sum of Y and o C pools, in which organic inputs (roots, residues and animal manure) enter the Y pool and input to the o pool is a fraction (h) of the decay of Y. Canada specific coefficients were developed for cereals and forages based on annual grain yield, harvest index and shoot-to-root ratios to calculate annual average above- and below- ground residue C input (Bolinder et al., 2007). Measured yields of forage, hay, and grain as well as forage utilization rate were used to estimate above- and below-ground C input. For perennial forages, we assumed an annual root-turnover of 50% (Kuzyakov and Schneckenberger, 2004). Application of C from on-farm produced manure of 0.27 Mg C ha yr to crop fields (barley and triticale) was estimated from the volume of manure stockpiled and its bulk density (79 g Pattey et al., 2005) and C content (175 g kg Hao et al., 2003). For grazing rangelands, half of the forage C consumed by grazing animals was assumed to be returned to the field through feces (fecal C; SCCC (Soil Conservation Council of Canada), 2001) and used as input to the Y pool in ICBM. Model simulations were initialized based on measured soil C contents (0-30 cm): 90.9 Mg ha 1 for natural ran- geland (Li et al., 2012), 27.9 Mg ha for re-established rangeland (Iwaasa et al., 2012) and 59.7 Mg ha for crops (barley and triticale; VandenBygaart et al., 2003). Since soil C cycle is a slow process that requires many years to reach equilibrium (Poeplau et al., 2011), ICBM was simulated over a 100-year period assuming that the simulated farm maintained the current management over several farming generations. The mean rate of soil C change from the 100-year simulation was used as an annual rate of soil C change over the 8-year production cycle.
37: Total farm GHG emissions were 10.2% greater for LC than HC and 7.5% greater for LCMR than HCMR grazing management. Greenhouse gas intensity of beef production varied among the grazing management scenarios ranging from 14.5-16.0 COe kg live weight and from 24.1-26.6 kg COe kg carcass weight. Intensity estimates decreased with increased stocking rate: HC grazing management had 9.2% lower GHG intensity than LC grazing system (14.5 vs 16.0 kg COe kg 1 live weight). of rotational grazing for the backgrounding operation in LCMR and HCMR had no effect on estimated intensity, although it influenced the distribution of emissions among sources (Table 7). Comparisons of ro- tational and continuous grazing on rangeland have been inconsistent: no difference in forage quality and animal performance in some studies

(Laycock and Conrad, 1981; Pitts and Bryant, 1987; Manley et al., 1997; Briske et al., 2008) but greater forage and animal productivity for rotational than continuous grazing in others (Walton et al., 1981; Willms et al., 1986; Briske et al., 2008).

other whole-farm modelling studies is difficult given the diversity of production systems, methodologies, assumptions and emissions factors used (Crosson et al., 2011). Greenhouse gas intensity estimates in our study were similar to those in previous studies of similar beef produc- tion systems (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Basarab et al., 2012). Basarab et al. (2012) conducted an LCA study based on an average annual production cycle and estimated GHG intensities of 11.6-12.6 COe kg live weight and 19.9-22.5 kg COe kg car- cass weight for beef production systems in western Canada. Using an approach similar to that in our study, Beauchemin et al. (2010) esti- mated a GHG intensity of 13 kg kg live weight and 21.7 kg CO2e kg carcass weight. Differences in factors such as the time of marketing feeder cattle, ADG and average body weight of ani- mals, diet quality, CH4 conversion factors, land use, amount of land required and stocking rate may have contributed to the observed 13-27% difference among GHG intensity estimates in our study and previous reported estimates. For example, in the study of Beauchemin et al. (2010), backgrounded cattle were fed a diet of 60% barley silage and 40% barley grain (high quality diet) with lower Ym (6.5), and feeder cattle were slaughtered at the age of 16.5 months, reaching market weight of 605 kg. When slaughter was extended to 20 months (621 kg) by increasing the use of forage (hay and pasture) in the backgrounding operation, GHG intensity is increased by 6.5% (Beauchemin et al., 2011). In our study, backgrounding lasted longer (286 vs 110 days) which extended the duration of animal slaughter to 20.6-21.2 months to reach the target market weight of 630 kg (Table 1). Furthermore, backgrounded cattle were managed on average-quality mixed hay and re-established rangeland with higher Ym (7.0, Table 5). For beef production in the upper Midwest United States (U.S.), Pelletier et al. (2010) estimated 8.6% greater GHG intensity for systems that managed backgrounded cattle on pasture before moving to a finishing operation with cattle slaughtered at the age of 21-23 months, compared to production systems that sent weaned calves directly to the feedlot with cattle harvested at the age of 16-17 months (16.2 kg COe kg live weight). A lower stocking rate (0.69 AUM ha-1 for cow-calf pairs and backgrounded cattle on ran- geland was used in Beauchemin et al. (2010), which may influence

forage quality and productivity, animal performance (Willms et al., 1986) and thus GHG emissions (Pinares-Patiño et al., 2007). Generally, GHG intensity (kg COe kg 1 carcass weight) of beef at the farm-gate has varied considerably among production systems (Crosson et al., 2011): 15.6-27.3 in Europe (Williams et al., 2006; Nguyen et al., 2010), 22.9-34.9 in the U.S. (Pelletier et al., 2010; Lupo et al., 2013) and 28-41 in Brazil (Cederberg et al., 2011; Mazzetto et al., 2015).

Several LCA studies examined grazing management as a means of reducing GHG emissions from rangeland-based beef production systems (Liebig et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2015; Church et al., 2015). Wang et al. (2015) evaluated the impact of continuous and rotational grazing strategies on total farm emissions for cow-calf production system and estimated lower emission intensity (COe per ha) for continuous grazing with low stocking rate relative to continuous grazing with high stocking rate and rotational grazing. However, when soil C sequestra- tion is taken into account, net GHG emissions of the cow-calf farm are negative for all grazing strategies. Furthermore, a whole-farm GHG analysis that compared high and moderate stocking rates based on long- term grazing on natural rangeland in the northern Great Plains of the U.S. reported that moderate stocking rate can have greater benefit in GHG mitigation (Liebig et al., 2010). They also observed that rangeland under medium and high stocking rates can be a net COe sink.

Comparisons of ro- tational and continuous grazing on rangeland have been inconsistent: no difference in forage quality and animal performance in some studies

other whole-farm modelling studies is difficult given the diversity of production systems, methodologies, assumptions and emissions factors used (Crosson et al., 2011). Greenhouse gas intensity estimates in our study were similar to those in previous studies of similar beef produc- tion systems (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Basarab et al., 2012). Basarab et al. (2012) conducted an LCA study based on an average annual production cycle and estimated GHG intensities of 11.6-12.6 COe kg live weight and 19.9-22.5 kg COe kg car- cass weight for beef production systems in western Canada. Using an approach similar to that in our study, Beauchemin et al. (2010) esti- mated a GHG intensity of 13 kg kg live weight and 21.7 kg CO2e kg carcass weight. Differences in factors such as the time of marketing feeder cattle, ADG and average body weight of ani- mals, diet quality, CH4 conversion factors, land use, amount of land required and stocking rate may have contributed to the observed 13-27% difference among GHG intensity estimates in our study and previous reported estimates. For example, in the study of Beauchemin et al. (2010), backgrounded cattle were fed a diet of 60% barley silage and 40% barley grain (high quality diet) with lower Ym (6.5), and feeder cattle were slaughtered at the age of 16.5 months, reaching market weight of 605 kg. When slaughter was extended to 20 months (621 kg) by increasing the use of forage (hay and pasture) in the backgrounding operation, GHG intensity is increased by 6.5% (Beauchemin et al., 2011). In our study, backgrounding lasted longer (286 vs 110 days) which extended the duration of animal slaughter to 20.6-21.2 months to reach the target market weight of 630 kg (Table 1). Furthermore, backgrounded cattle were managed on average-quality mixed hay and re-established rangeland with higher Ym (7.0, Table 5). For beef production in the upper Midwest United States (U.S.), Pelletier et al. (2010) estimated 8.6% greater GHG intensity for systems that managed backgrounded cattle on pasture before moving to a finishing operation with cattle slaughtered at the age of 21-23 months, compared to production systems that sent weaned calves directly to the feedlot with cattle harvested at the age of 16-17 months (16.2 kg COe kg live weight). A lower stocking rate (0.69 AUM ha-1 for cow-calf pairs and backgrounded cattle on ran- geland was used in Beauchemin et al. (2010), which may influence

forage quality and productivity, animal performance (Willms et al., 1986) and thus GHG emissions (Pinares-Patiño et al., 2007). Generally, GHG intensity (kg COe kg 1 carcass weight) of beef at the farm-gate has varied considerably among production systems (Crosson et al., 2011): 15.6-27.3 in Europe (Williams et al., 2006; Nguyen et al., 2010), 22.9-34.9 in the U.S. (Pelletier et al., 2010; Lupo et al., 2013) and 28-41 in Brazil (Cederberg et al., 2011; Mazzetto et al., 2015).

Several LCA studies examined grazing management as a means of reducing GHG emissions from rangeland-based beef production systems (Liebig et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2015; Church et al., 2015). Wang et al. (2015) evaluated the impact of continuous and rotational grazing strategies on total farm emissions for cow-calf production system and estimated lower emission intensity (COe per ha) for continuous grazing with low stocking rate relative to continuous grazing with high stocking rate and rotational grazing. However, when soil C sequestra- tion is taken into account, net GHG emissions of the cow-calf farm are negative for all grazing strategies. Furthermore, a whole-farm GHG analysis that compared high and moderate stocking rates based on long- term grazing on natural rangeland in the northern Great Plains of the U.S. reported that moderate stocking rate can have greater benefit in GHG mitigation (Liebig et al., 2010). They also observed that rangeland under medium and high stocking rates can be a net COe sink.

Using an approach similar to that in our study, Beauchemin et al. (2010) esti- mated a GHG intensity of 13 kg kg live weight and 21.7 kg CO2e kg carcass weight. Differences in factors such as the time of marketing feeder cattle, ADG and average body weight of ani- mals, diet quality, CH4 conversion factors, land use, amount of land required and stocking rate may have contributed to the observed 13-27% difference among GHG intensity estimates in our study and previous reported estimates. For example, in the study of Beauchemin et al. (2010), backgrounded cattle were fed a diet of 60% barley silage and 40% barley grain (high quality diet) with lower Ym (6.5), and feeder cattle were slaughtered at the age of 16.5 months, reaching market weight of 605 kg. When slaughter was extended to 20 months (621 kg) by increasing the use of forage (hay and pasture) in the backgrounding operation, GHG intensity is increased by 6.5% (Beauchemin et al., 2011). In our study, backgrounding lasted longer (286 vs 110 days) which extended the duration of animal slaughter to 20.6-21.2 months to reach the target market weight of 630 kg (Table 1). Furthermore, backgrounded cattle were managed on average-quality mixed hay and re-established rangeland with higher Ym (7.0, Table 5). For beef production in the upper Midwest United States (U.S.), Pelletier et al. (2010) estimated 8.6% greater GHG intensity for systems that managed backgrounded cattle on pasture before moving to a finishing operation with cattle slaughtered at the age of 21-23 months, compared to production systems that sent weaned calves directly to the feedlot with cattle harvested at the age of 16-17 months (16.2 kg COe kg live weight). A lower stocking rate (0.69 AUM ha-1 for cow-calf pairs and backgrounded cattle on ran- geland was used in Beauchemin et al. (2010), which may influence

forage quality and productivity, animal performance (Willms et al., 1986) and thus GHG emissions (Pinares-Patiño et al., 2007). Generally, GHG intensity (kg COe kg 1 carcass weight) of beef at the farm-gate has varied considerably among production systems (Crosson et al., 2011): 15.6-27.3 in Europe (Williams et al., 2006; Nguyen et al., 2010), 22.9-34.9 in the U.S. (Pelletier et al., 2010; Lupo et al., 2013) and 28-41 in Brazil (Cederberg et al., 2011; Mazzetto et al., 2015).

Several LCA studies examined grazing management as a means of reducing GHG emissions from rangeland-based beef production systems (Liebig et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2015; Church et al., 2015). Wang et al. (2015) evaluated the impact of continuous and rotational grazing strategies on total farm emissions for cow-calf production system and estimated lower emission intensity (COe per ha) for continuous grazing with low stocking rate relative to continuous grazing with high stocking rate and rotational grazing. However, when soil C sequestra- tion is taken into account, net GHG emissions of the cow-calf farm are negative for all grazing strategies. Furthermore, a whole-farm GHG analysis that compared high and moderate stocking rates based on long- term grazing on natural rangeland in the northern Great Plains of the U.S. reported that moderate stocking rate can have greater benefit in GHG mitigation (Liebig et al., 2010). They also observed that rangeland under medium and high stocking rates can be a net COe sink.

A deferred-rotational system was used as the rotational grazing strategy. b Beef produced (Mg) was sourced from culled cows and bulls and slaughtered steers and heifers. other whole-farm modelling studies is difficult given the diversity of production systems, methodologies, assumptions and emissions factors used (Crosson et al., 2011). Greenhouse gas intensity estimates in our study were similar to those in previous studies of similar beef produc- tion systems (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Basarab et al., 2012). Basarab et al. (2012) conducted an LCA study based on an average annual production cycle and estimated GHG intensities of 11.6-12.6 COe kg live weight and 19.9-22.5 kg COe kg car- cass weight for beef production systems in western Canada. Using an approach similar to that in our study, Beauchemin et al. (2010) esti- mated a GHG intensity of 13 kg kg live weight and 21.7 kg CO2e kg carcass weight. Differences in factors such as the time of marketing feeder cattle, ADG and average body weight of ani- mals, diet quality, CH4 conversion factors, land use, amount of land required and stocking rate may have contributed to the observed 13-27% difference among GHG intensity estimates in our study and previous reported estimates. For example, in the study of Beauchemin et al. (2010), backgrounded cattle were fed a diet of 60% barley silage and 40% barley grain (high quality diet) with lower Ym (6.5), and feeder cattle were slaughtered at the age of 16.5 months, reaching market weight of 605 kg. When slaughter was extended to 20 months (621 kg) by increasing the use of forage (hay and pasture) in the backgrounding operation, GHG intensity is increased by 6.5% (Beauchemin et al., 2011). In our study, backgrounding lasted longer (286 vs 110 days) which extended the duration of animal slaughter to 20.6-21.2 months to reach the target market weight of 630 kg (Table 1). Furthermore, backgrounded cattle were managed on average-quality mixed hay and re-established rangeland with higher Ym (7.0, Table 5). For beef production in the upper Midwest United States (U.S.), Pelletier et al. (2010) estimated 8.6% greater GHG intensity for systems that managed backgrounded cattle on pasture before moving to a finishing operation with cattle slaughtered at the age of 21-23 months, compared to production systems that sent weaned calves directly to the feedlot with cattle harvested at the age of 16-17 months (16.2 kg COe kg live weight). A lower stocking rate (0.69 AUM ha-1 for cow-calf pairs and backgrounded cattle on ran- geland was used in Beauchemin et al. (2010), which may influence

forage quality and productivity, animal performance (Willms et al., 1986) and thus GHG emissions (Pinares-Patiño et al., 2007). Generally, GHG intensity (kg COe kg 1 carcass weight) of beef at the farm-gate has varied considerably among production systems (Crosson et al., 2011): 15.6-27.3 in Europe (Williams et al., 2006; Nguyen et al., 2010), 22.9-34.9 in the U.S. (Pelletier et al., 2010; Lupo et al., 2013) and 28-41 in Brazil (Cederberg et al., 2011; Mazzetto et al., 2015).

Several LCA studies examined grazing management as a means of reducing GHG emissions from rangeland-based beef production systems (Liebig et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2015; Church et al., 2015). Wang et al. (2015) evaluated the impact of continuous and rotational grazing strategies on total farm emissions for cow-calf production system and estimated lower emission intensity (COe per ha) for continuous grazing with low stocking rate relative to continuous grazing with high stocking rate and rotational grazing. However, when soil C sequestra- tion is taken into account, net GHG emissions of the cow-calf farm are negative for all grazing strategies. Furthermore, a whole-farm GHG analysis that compared high and moderate stocking rates based on long- term grazing on natural rangeland in the northern Great Plains of the U.S. reported that moderate stocking rate can have greater benefit in GHG mitigation (Liebig et al., 2010). They also observed that rangeland under medium and high stocking rates can be a net COe sink.

of rotational grazing for the backgrounding operation in LCMR and HCMR had no effect on estimated intensity, although it influenced the distribution of emissions among sources (Table 7). Comparisons of ro- tational and continuous grazing on rangeland have been inconsistent: no difference in forage quality and animal performance in some studies

(Laycock and Conrad, 1981; Pitts and Bryant, 1987; Manley et al., 1997; Briske et al., 2008) but greater forage and animal productivity for rotational than continuous grazing in others (Walton et al., 1981; Willms et al., 1986; Briske et al., 2008).

other whole-farm modelling studies is difficult given the diversity of production systems, methodologies, assumptions and emissions factors used (Crosson et al., 2011). Greenhouse gas intensity estimates in our study were similar to those in previous studies of similar beef produc- tion systems (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Basarab et al., 2012). Basarab et al. (2012) conducted an LCA study based on an average annual production cycle and estimated GHG intensities of 11.6-12.6 COe kg live weight and 19.9-22.5 kg COe kg car- cass weight for beef production systems in western Canada. Using an approach similar to that in our study, Beauchemin et al. (2010) esti- mated a GHG intensity of 13 kg kg live weight and 21.7 kg CO2e kg carcass weight. Differences in factors such as the time of marketing feeder cattle, ADG and average body weight of ani- mals, diet quality, CH4 conversion factors, land use, amount of land required and stocking rate may have contributed to the observed 13-27% difference among GHG intensity estimates in our study and previous reported estimates. For example, in the study of Beauchemin et al. (2010), backgrounded cattle were fed a diet of 60% barley silage and 40% barley grain (high quality diet) with lower Ym (6.5), and feeder cattle were slaughtered at the age of 16.5 months, reaching market weight of 605 kg. When slaughter was extended to 20 months (621 kg) by increasing the use of forage (hay and pasture) in the backgrounding operation, GHG intensity is increased by 6.5% (Beauchemin et al., 2011). In our study, backgrounding lasted longer (286 vs 110 days) which extended the duration of animal slaughter to 20.6-21.2 months to reach the target market weight of 630 kg (Table 1). Furthermore, backgrounded cattle were managed on average-quality mixed hay and re-established rangeland with higher Ym (7.0, Table 5). For beef production in the upper Midwest United States (U.S.), Pelletier et al. (2010) estimated 8.6% greater GHG intensity for systems that managed backgrounded cattle on pasture before moving to a finishing operation with cattle slaughtered at the age of 21-23 months, compared to production systems that sent weaned calves directly to the feedlot with cattle harvested at the age of 16-17 months (16.2 kg COe kg live weight). A lower stocking rate (0.69 AUM ha-1 for cow-calf pairs and backgrounded cattle on ran- geland was used in Beauchemin et al. (2010), which may influence

forage quality and productivity, animal performance (Willms et al., 1986) and thus GHG emissions (Pinares-Patiño et al., 2007). Generally, GHG intensity (kg COe kg 1 carcass weight) of beef at the farm-gate has varied considerably among production systems (Crosson et al., 2011): 15.6-27.3 in Europe (Williams et al., 2006; Nguyen et al., 2010), 22.9-34.9 in the U.S. (Pelletier et al., 2010; Lupo et al., 2013) and 28-41 in Brazil (Cederberg et al., 2011; Mazzetto et al., 2015).

Several LCA studies examined grazing management as a means of reducing GHG emissions from rangeland-based beef production systems (Liebig et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2015; Church et al., 2015). Wang et al. (2015) evaluated the impact of continuous and rotational grazing strategies on total farm emissions for cow-calf production system and estimated lower emission intensity (COe per ha) for continuous grazing with low stocking rate relative to continuous grazing with high stocking rate and rotational grazing. However, when soil C sequestra- tion is taken into account, net GHG emissions of the cow-calf farm are negative for all grazing strategies. Furthermore, a whole-farm GHG analysis that compared high and moderate stocking rates based on long- term grazing on natural rangeland in the northern Great Plains of the U.S. reported that moderate stocking rate can have greater benefit in GHG mitigation (Liebig et al., 2010). They also observed that rangeland under medium and high stocking rates can be a net COe sink.
52: Categorizing total farm emissions by source indicated that regard- less of the grazing management practice, enteric CH4 contributed 67-68% of total emissions, followed by manure N2O emissions (14-16%) and manure CH4 (9-10%) with the remaining emissions from soil/cropping N2O and energy use CO2 (Fig. 2). Variation in propor- tional contribution among grazing management scenarios was smaller for the largest contributors, such as enteric CH4 (0.4-2.1%) and emis- sions from manure management (N , 1-9%; CH4, 1-7%), than those of soil/cropping N20 (3-14%) and energy use CO2 (3-23%) which con- tributed little to total farm emissions. Proportional contribution ranging from 53 to 63% for enteric CH4 and 20-23% for manure N2O has been reported for Canadian beef production systems (Beauchemin et al., 2010; Basarab et al., 2012). It should be noted that in addition to dif- ferences in management practices among studies (e.g., extended grazing on swathed cereal crops in our study), the use of different character- ization factors to calculate global warming potential of CH4 and N2O (Myhre et al., 2013) contributed to differences.

Grazing management affects the plant community and rangeland productivity, quality and digestibility, which in turn affect animal performance (Willms et al., 1986; McCollum et al., 1999) and enteric CH4 emissions (Johnson and Johnson, 1995; Reeder and Schuman, 2002). Weight gain per head generally deceased as stocking rate in- creased (Willms et al., 1986) and was lower for HC than LC grazing (Table 2). However, in terms of quality, rangeland under HC grazing management had greater digestible energy and CP and lower NDF than that under LC grazing (Table 5) which decreased CH4 emissions from enteric fermentation (Table 7). Total enteric CH4 emission was 7-15% lower in the HC and HCMR management scenarios than the LC and LCMR scenarios. Pinares-Patiño et al. (2007) reported that pasture managed under low stocking rate has lower quality and digestibility and greater enteric CH4 emissions per unit of digestible feed intake than pasture managed under high stocking rate. Furthermore, in southern mixed grass prairie, Heitschmidt et al. (1989) compared moderate and heavy stocking rates under continuous grazing based on a 25-year grazing study and reported higher quality (CP and organic matter di- gestibility) and lower productivity for rangeland under heavy than moderate stocking rate. Overall, these results also underline the im- portance of improving grass quality in reducing enteric CH4 emission, and in return, total farm GHG emission.
When total GHG emissions were categorized by animal groups, cow- calf pairs were the main contributor (52-54%) followed by

backgrounded cattle (21-22%), replacement stock (cow and bull calves, growing heifers and bulls and nursing cows at the end of their life cycle milking replacement calves, 16%) and finishers (8-11%, Fig. 3). Pro- portional contribution from finishers was greater for HC and HCMR (11%) and varied more (4-30%) among scenarios than those of the other animal categories (0.3-7%). These differences are related to dif- ferences in ADG of calves and backgrounded cattle on rangeland among the grazing management scenarios (Table 2) that resulted in body weight differences at the end of the backgrounding phase. Since initial body weight in the finishing operation varied among grazing manage- ment scenarios, the number of days until animals reached the target market weight (630 kg) differed. The HC and HCMR scenarios had 13-16% lower ADG for calves and backgrounded cattle on re-stablished rangeland, which led to an additional 15-23 days in the finishing op- eration compared to the LC and LCMR scenarios, which required 121 and 129 days, respectively. The LC scenario required 35% more rangeland area than the HC scenario (441 ha) to produce the same amount of beef, whereas the LCMR sce- nario required 26% more rangeland than the HCMR scenario (463 ha,

Fig. 3. Proportional contribution (COe, % of total greenhouse gas emissions) of animal categories on the farm for the grazing management scenarios: light continuous grazing (LC), heavy continuous grazing (HC), light continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate deferred-rotational grazing for back- grounded cattle (LCMR), and heavy continuous grazing for cow- calf pairs and moderate deferred-rotational grazing for back- grounded cattle (HCMR). A deferred-rotation system was used as the rotational grazing strategy. "Breeding stock-not breeding" were replacement stock that included cow and bull calves, growing heifers and bulls and nursing cows at the end of their life cycle milking replacement calves.

Table 4). Although the HC scenario had twice the stocking rate of the LC scenario, the latter required only 35% more rangeland (instead of 100%) because its natural rangeland had higher forage production (Table 4), which lengthened the grazing season and in turn decreased the area of rangeland it required.

In terms of production efficiency, measured as kg beef (live or carcass weight) per ha, the HC scenario was 46% more efficient than the LC scenario (66 vs 45 kg carcass weight ha-1), whereas the HCMR scenario was 30% more efficient than the LCMR scenario (64 vs 49 kg carcass weight ha Table 7). Differences in farm efficiency among the grazing management scenarios were due to differences in the total amount of land they required. Beauchemin et al. (2010) and Basarab et al. (2012) estimated greater efficiencies: 107 and 140 kg carcass ha respectively. However, their production system relied less on rangeland (237-255 ha) and more on cropland (grain and silage, 37-95 ha) than that in our study. Studies have reported that production efficiency (live weight produced per unit area) increased as stocking rate increased (Willms et al., 1986; McCollum et al., 1999); however, the increase in beef production under heavy grazing could decrease the long-term economic and ecological sustainability of the farm and the rangeland, respectively. Our study, like others (e.g., Liebig et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2015), has not considered fully the resulting implications for long-term sustainability (i.e., economic, ecological and social; Janzen, 2011) of these systems. For example, LC grazing man- agement, which requires more rangeland, may maintain long-term health of the grazing ecosystem better than HC management, which produces beef more efficiently in the short-term.
