60: On average, native rangeland used for cow-calf pairs under LC grazing management sequestered C at a rate of 0.062 Mgha~1-1, whereas under HC grazing management it sequestered C at a rate of 0.01 Mg However, for the re-established rangeland used for backgrounded cattle, the rate of soil C sequestration was greater under HC and deferred-rotational grazing management (0.126 Mgha~1yr-1 than under LC grazing management (0.066 Mg ha This differ- ence could be due to the higher forage yield of re-established rangeland under HC and deferred-rotational grazing, which increased the amount of residue and feces C returned to the soil than LC grazing (Table 4). Semmartin et al. (2008) reported that grazing livestock accelerates re- turn of C and N to the soil by depositing fecal and urine, potentially increasing net primary productivity. In the HC and LC scenarios, fecal C accounted for 25% and 16%, respectively, of the total C entering the Y pool. Baron et al. (2002) observed that fecal C contributed 68% and 42% of total above-ground C returning to the soil under heavy and light stocking rates, respectively. Predicted soil C sequestration values in our study lie within the range of previously reported values based on long- term grazing studies (Reeder and Schuman, 2002; Derner and Schuman, 2007; Wang et al., 2014). Wang et al. (2014) examined effects of grassland management on soil C sequestration for Canadian grassland grazed in recent decades by compiling measurements from experiments. They reported that the rate of soil C change for grasslands managed under light grazing varied from 0.66 (loss) to 0.77 (gain) while those for grasslands under heavy grazing varied from 1.14 (loss) to 1.58 (gain) Mg ha yr Since all on-farm manure produced was applied to the land, the predicted soil C seques- tration (0.07-0.46 Mg under crop fields (triticale and barley; Table 8) could be related to the addition of C (0.27 Mg from manure. Hao et al. (2003) conducted a long-term (25 years) study of a crop field in southern Alberta to investigate the response of soil C following cattle manure application and observed a linear relationship (r2 = 0.93) in which soil C content (0-15 cm) increased by 0.181 g kg - 1 for every ton of manure C added. Izaurralde et al. (1997) and Grant et al. (2001) also reported similar results.

a LC = light continuous grazing, HC = heavy continuous grazing, LCMR = light con- tinuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate deferred-rotational grazing for back- grounded cattle, HCMR = heavy continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate deferred-rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle. A deferred-rotational system was used as the rotational grazing strategy. b Re-established rangeland was established in 2001 by seeding a mixture of native cool-season grass, native legume species, native warm-season grass and native shrub species (Schellenberg et al., 2012). C Soil carbon (C) was converted into CO2 assuming that 1 kg soil organic C = 3.667 kg CO2, Negative values indicated soil C gain or CO2 sequestration.

been highlighted in several studies (e.g., Soussana et al., 2007, 2010; Pelletier et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2015). However, estimating impacts of grazing management on soil C sequestration in grazing rangelands is complex because C dynamics in grasslands involve complex interactions of climate, soil, plant communities and management (Derner and Schuman, 2007; Pineiro et al., 2010). Overall, grazing management influences soil C sequestration/loss through its impacts on net primary productivity, fate of plant residues, plant community structure, redis- tribution of nutrients through manure deposition and influence of nu- trient cycling through soil-plant-animal systems (Baron et al., 2002; Li et al., 2012, 2012b; Wang et al., 2014). Grazing generally reduces the standing dead and surface litters, while animal traffic enhances physical breakdown and incorporation of plant residues into surface soil, which in turn affect soil physical and chemical properties (Manley et al., 1995; Reeder and Schuman, 2002; Teague et al., 2011). Furthermore, grazing management affects soil N through its impact on organic N miner- alization (Dormaar et al., 1990) and soil water dynamics by altering infiltration rate (Naeth et al., 1990, 1991), which has a direct impact on net primary productivity. However, inconsistent impacts of grazing management on soil C have been reported (Naeth et al., 1991; Willms et al., 2002; Li et al., 2012). Milchunas and Lauenroth (1993) reviewed

236 studies and found a nearly equal number of positive and negative impacts of grazing on soil C. Similarly, studying long-term grazing of rangeland in central Alberta, Li et al. (2012) reported no change in soil C for ungrazed, moderately stocked or highly stocked rangeland. Manley et al. (1995) also found no difference in C sequestration among continuous and rotational (deferred-rotational) grazing systems under heavy stocking. Conversely, Naeth et al. (1991) observed that Canadian fescue prairie under light and heavy grazing for 46 years sequestered 2-19 Mg ha 1 soil C than ungrazed grassland. Furthermore, in a 56- year study, Reeder and Schuman (2002) observed significantly greater soil C under heavily grazed rangeland than lightly grazed or ungrazed rangeland. Overall, the lack of consistent impact of grazing manage- ment on soil C among studies can be attributed to the complexity of grassland systems and difficulties of measuring soil C change in ran- gelands with often-variable soil C stocks (Ellert et al., 2001; Conant et al., 2010). In our study, the lower sequestration for rangeland under the HC and HCMR management scenarios could be due to lower pro- ductivity (Table 4), which could decrease C input to the soil. For overgrazed and poorly managed rangelands, Su et al. (2005) indicated that lower soil C contents have been attributed to rangeland with less primary production. Furthermore, Allen et al. (2013) reported that soil C is strongly influenced by total standing DM, soil type and the type of dominant grass species. Different rates of soil C sequestration have been reported for the three main grass mixtures in Canadian Prairies (Wang et al., 2014): dry mixed (0.05 Mg C ha 1 yr mixed grasses (0.16 Mg yr and fescue Mg C ha 1 yr Natural rangeland, used for cow-calf pairs in our study was dominated by foothills rough fescue grass and parry oat grass/bluebunch fescue in the lightly and heavily grazed areas, respectively (Dormaar and Willms, 1990).

When soil C sequestration was included in the farm GHG analysis, net emissions per unit beef and per unit area decreased by 12-25% and 13-32%, respectively, for the grazing management scenarios (Table 8). The largest reduction was observed for the LC (22%) and LCMR (24%) scenarios because the system sequestered more CO2 (107-109 M ha than the HC and HCMR scenarios, which sequestered 94-105 Mg ha yr (Table 8). The reduc- tion in GHG due to C sequestration was less for the HC and HCMR (12-13%) scenarios because of the lower rate of soil C sequestration by the natural rangeland under heavy stocking and because less land was needed for the HC and HCMR systems (Table 8). Relatively few beef LCA studies have included soil C sequestration when estimating farm GHG emissions (Crosson et al., 2011). However, studies estimated that inclusion of C sequestration reduced whole-farm GHG emissions by 10-43% (Phetteplace et al., 2001; Basarab et al., 2012; Lupo et al., 2013) or even offset all GHG emissions and made the farm a net GHG sink (Liebig et al., 2010; Beauchemin et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2015). Such offsets, however, can occur only until soil C reaches equilibrium. For Canadian beef production, Basarab et al. (2012) estimated a de- crease of 11-16% in farm GHG emission intensity by including soil C sequestration, smaller than the decrease in our study. The difference could be attributed to differences in forage yield, grazing area required and stocking rate. For Midwest U.S. beef production, Pelletier et al. (2010) estimated that potential C sequestration could offset 43% of GHG emissions from the modelled grazing system. Generally, GHG in- tensity decreases when soil C sequestration estimates are included in analysis of total farm GHG emissions under different grazing manage- ment scenarios, which underlines the importance of considering both GHG emissions and C sequestration simultaneously in farm LCA.

a LC = light continuous grazing, HC = heavy continuous grazing, LCMR = light con- tinuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate deferred-rotational grazing for back- grounded cattle, HCMR = heavy continuous grazing for cow-calf pairs and moderate deferred-rotational grazing for backgrounded cattle. A deferred-rotational system was used as the rotational grazing strategy. b Re-established rangeland was established in 2001 by seeding a mixture of native cool-season grass, native legume species, native warm-season grass and native shrub species (Schellenberg et al., 2012). C Soil carbon (C) was converted into CO2 assuming that 1 kg soil organic C = 3.667 kg CO2, Negative values indicated soil C gain or CO2 sequestration.

been highlighted in several studies (e.g., Soussana et al., 2007, 2010; Pelletier et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2015). However, estimating impacts of grazing management on soil C sequestration in grazing rangelands is complex because C dynamics in grasslands involve complex interactions of climate, soil, plant communities and management (Derner and Schuman, 2007; Pineiro et al., 2010). Overall, grazing management influences soil C sequestration/loss through its impacts on net primary productivity, fate of plant residues, plant community structure, redis- tribution of nutrients through manure deposition and influence of nu- trient cycling through soil-plant-animal systems (Baron et al., 2002; Li et al., 2012, 2012b; Wang et al., 2014). Grazing generally reduces the standing dead and surface litters, while animal traffic enhances physical breakdown and incorporation of plant residues into surface soil, which in turn affect soil physical and chemical properties (Manley et al., 1995; Reeder and Schuman, 2002; Teague et al., 2011). Furthermore, grazing management affects soil N through its impact on organic N miner- alization (Dormaar et al., 1990) and soil water dynamics by altering infiltration rate (Naeth et al., 1990, 1991), which has a direct impact on net primary productivity. However, inconsistent impacts of grazing management on soil C have been reported (Naeth et al., 1991; Willms et al., 2002; Li et al., 2012). Milchunas and Lauenroth (1993) reviewed

236 studies and found a nearly equal number of positive and negative impacts of grazing on soil C. Similarly, studying long-term grazing of rangeland in central Alberta, Li et al. (2012) reported no change in soil C for ungrazed, moderately stocked or highly stocked rangeland. Manley et al. (1995) also found no difference in C sequestration among continuous and rotational (deferred-rotational) grazing systems under heavy stocking. Conversely, Naeth et al. (1991) observed that Canadian fescue prairie under light and heavy grazing for 46 years sequestered 2-19 Mg ha 1 soil C than ungrazed grassland. Furthermore, in a 56- year study, Reeder and Schuman (2002) observed significantly greater soil C under heavily grazed rangeland than lightly grazed or ungrazed rangeland. Overall, the lack of consistent impact of grazing manage- ment on soil C among studies can be attributed to the complexity of grassland systems and difficulties of measuring soil C change in ran- gelands with often-variable soil C stocks (Ellert et al., 2001; Conant et al., 2010). In our study, the lower sequestration for rangeland under the HC and HCMR management scenarios could be due to lower pro- ductivity (Table 4), which could decrease C input to the soil. For overgrazed and poorly managed rangelands, Su et al. (2005) indicated that lower soil C contents have been attributed to rangeland with less primary production. Furthermore, Allen et al. (2013) reported that soil C is strongly influenced by total standing DM, soil type and the type of dominant grass species. Different rates of soil C sequestration have been reported for the three main grass mixtures in Canadian Prairies (Wang et al., 2014): dry mixed (0.05 Mg C ha 1 yr mixed grasses (0.16 Mg yr and fescue Mg C ha 1 yr Natural rangeland, used for cow-calf pairs in our study was dominated by foothills rough fescue grass and parry oat grass/bluebunch fescue in the lightly and heavily grazed areas, respectively (Dormaar and Willms, 1990).

When soil C sequestration was included in the farm GHG analysis, net emissions per unit beef and per unit area decreased by 12-25% and 13-32%, respectively, for the grazing management scenarios (Table 8). The largest reduction was observed for the LC (22%) and LCMR (24%) scenarios because the system sequestered more CO2 (107-109 M ha than the HC and HCMR scenarios, which sequestered 94-105 Mg ha yr (Table 8). The reduc- tion in GHG due to C sequestration was less for the HC and HCMR (12-13%) scenarios because of the lower rate of soil C sequestration by the natural rangeland under heavy stocking and because less land was needed for the HC and HCMR systems (Table 8). Relatively few beef LCA studies have included soil C sequestration when estimating farm GHG emissions (Crosson et al., 2011). However, studies estimated that inclusion of C sequestration reduced whole-farm GHG emissions by 10-43% (Phetteplace et al., 2001; Basarab et al., 2012; Lupo et al., 2013) or even offset all GHG emissions and made the farm a net GHG sink (Liebig et al., 2010; Beauchemin et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2015). Such offsets, however, can occur only until soil C reaches equilibrium. For Canadian beef production, Basarab et al. (2012) estimated a de- crease of 11-16% in farm GHG emission intensity by including soil C sequestration, smaller than the decrease in our study. The difference could be attributed to differences in forage yield, grazing area required and stocking rate. For Midwest U.S. beef production, Pelletier et al. (2010) estimated that potential C sequestration could offset 43% of GHG emissions from the modelled grazing system. Generally, GHG in- tensity decreases when soil C sequestration estimates are included in analysis of total farm GHG emissions under different grazing manage- ment scenarios, which underlines the importance of considering both GHG emissions and C sequestration simultaneously in farm LCA.
65: Annual rate of soil carbon sequestration estimated by the Introductory Carbon Balance Model, total land areas used for feed production within the farm over an 8-year pro- duction cycle and its associated CO2 sequestration and greenhouse gas intensity estimates (including soil carbon change).

However, inconsistent impacts of grazing management on soil C have been reported (Naeth et al., 1991; Willms et al., 2002; Li et al., 2012). Milchunas and Lauenroth (1993) reviewed

236 studies and found a nearly equal number of positive and negative impacts of grazing on soil C. Similarly, studying long-term grazing of rangeland in central Alberta, Li et al. (2012) reported no change in soil C for ungrazed, moderately stocked or highly stocked rangeland. Manley et al. (1995) also found no difference in C sequestration among continuous and rotational (deferred-rotational) grazing systems under heavy stocking. Conversely, Naeth et al. (1991) observed that Canadian fescue prairie under light and heavy grazing for 46 years sequestered 2-19 Mg ha 1 soil C than ungrazed grassland. Furthermore, in a 56- year study, Reeder and Schuman (2002) observed significantly greater soil C under heavily grazed rangeland than lightly grazed or ungrazed rangeland. Overall, the lack of consistent impact of grazing manage- ment on soil C among studies can be attributed to the complexity of grassland systems and difficulties of measuring soil C change in ran- gelands with often-variable soil C stocks (Ellert et al., 2001; Conant et al., 2010). In our study, the lower sequestration for rangeland under the HC and HCMR management scenarios could be due to lower pro- ductivity (Table 4), which could decrease C input to the soil. For overgrazed and poorly managed rangelands, Su et al. (2005) indicated that lower soil C contents have been attributed to rangeland with less primary production. Furthermore, Allen et al. (2013) reported that soil C is strongly influenced by total standing DM, soil type and the type of dominant grass species. Different rates of soil C sequestration have been reported for the three main grass mixtures in Canadian Prairies (Wang et al., 2014): dry mixed (0.05 Mg C ha 1 yr mixed grasses (0.16 Mg yr and fescue Mg C ha 1 yr Natural rangeland, used for cow-calf pairs in our study was dominated by foothills rough fescue grass and parry oat grass/bluebunch fescue in the lightly and heavily grazed areas, respectively (Dormaar and Willms, 1990).

When soil C sequestration was included in the farm GHG analysis, net emissions per unit beef and per unit area decreased by 12-25% and 13-32%, respectively, for the grazing management scenarios (Table 8). The largest reduction was observed for the LC (22%) and LCMR (24%) scenarios because the system sequestered more CO2 (107-109 M ha than the HC and HCMR scenarios, which sequestered 94-105 Mg ha yr (Table 8). The reduc- tion in GHG due to C sequestration was less for the HC and HCMR (12-13%) scenarios because of the lower rate of soil C sequestration by the natural rangeland under heavy stocking and because less land was needed for the HC and HCMR systems (Table 8). Relatively few beef LCA studies have included soil C sequestration when estimating farm GHG emissions (Crosson et al., 2011). However, studies estimated that inclusion of C sequestration reduced whole-farm GHG emissions by 10-43% (Phetteplace et al., 2001; Basarab et al., 2012; Lupo et al., 2013) or even offset all GHG emissions and made the farm a net GHG sink (Liebig et al., 2010; Beauchemin et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2015). Such offsets, however, can occur only until soil C reaches equilibrium. For Canadian beef production, Basarab et al. (2012) estimated a de- crease of 11-16% in farm GHG emission intensity by including soil C sequestration, smaller than the decrease in our study. The difference could be attributed to differences in forage yield, grazing area required and stocking rate. For Midwest U.S. beef production, Pelletier et al. (2010) estimated that potential C sequestration could offset 43% of GHG emissions from the modelled grazing system. Generally, GHG in- tensity decreases when soil C sequestration estimates are included in analysis of total farm GHG emissions under different grazing manage- ment scenarios, which underlines the importance of considering both GHG emissions and C sequestration simultaneously in farm LCA.

69: Grazing management had an impact on GHG intensity of beef pro- duction through its influence on enteric CH4 emissions (diet quality), animal performance (ADG) and soil C change. The HC and HCMR grazing management scenarios had lower GHG intensity and greater

production efficiency (kg beef per unit ha) than the LC and LCMR scenarios. Regardless of the grazing scenario, enteric fermentation and manure management contributed most to total farm GHG emissions. Including soil C sequestration in farm GHG analysis reduced farm GHG emissions by 12-25%, and the largest reduction was observed in the LC and LCMR scenarios since soil C sequestration under natural rangeland (grazed by cow-calf pairs) was less in the HC and HCMR scenarios. This emphasizes the importance of accounting for all emission sources and sinks within beef production systems when estimating their environ- mental impacts, and demonstrates the value of model-based LCA in addressing this objective. Furthermore, beyond supplying food and fiber, rangelands have other contributions such as provision of bene- ficial ecological goods and services that needs to be incorporated in LCA of beef production.
